# Module Assembler Development Guidelines

## Project Description

Module Assembler is a PowerShell Module, created using PowerShell v7, that has the purpose of templating new PowerShell modules and providing utilities to assist in PowerShell module development.

The project follows [SemVer](https://semver.org/) for versioning and the ChangeLog standards as outlined in [keepachangelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/). Versions are incremented by the function Update-MAModuleVersion.

The Module Assembler module is used to develop, test, and build itself (bootstrapping). Bootstrapping is achieved with the script Invoke-ModuleManualTest at the project root. Only when making changes to file Invoke-ModuleManualTest, warn the user in the Chat response that they should perform manual validation to ensure it still functions as expected.

### Project File Structure

- **src/public/** — Exported public cmdlets (noun portion of name prefixed with MA).
- **src/private/** — Internal helper functions used by public cmdlets.
- **src/classes/** — PowerShell class definitions.
- **src/resources/** — Templates, schemas, and resources used for generating new modules.
- **tests/** — Pester test files organized by type (Unit, Quality Assurance).
- **docs/** — Documentation files for public cmdlets. The contents are generated by the function Build-MAModuleDocumentation.
- **assets/** — Project-related assets.
- **.github/instructions/** — Coding standards and guidelines for the project.

## Project Coding Standards

- Follow the standards in the following files. If these files are not available in context and cannot be read from the workspace, inform the user which specific file(s) are missing and ask them to provide the content before proceeding with code generation.
  - [PowerShell instructions](instructions/powershell.instructions.md)
  - [Pester instructions](instructions/pester.instructions.md)
  - [Markdown instructions](instructions/markdown.instructions.md)
- Prefer readable, straightforward implementations. Add complexity only when required for input validation, error handling, security, or reducing long-term maintenance burden (e.g., eliminating duplicated logic -- For instance, extracting a shared 3-line pattern into a helper is acceptable; introducing a factory pattern for a single use case is not). Avoid premature abstraction and unnecessary design patterns.
- All public functions must use [CmdletBinding()] and validate all parameters using built-in validation attributes (e.g., [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()], [ValidateSet()], [ValidatePattern()]) where applicable. Use custom validation logic only when validation depends on multiple parameters together, runtime state (e.g., file existence, service availability), or conditions that built-in attributes cannot express. Functions that modify state must support -WhatIf via SupportsShouldProcess.

  **SupportsShouldProcess Decision Table:**

  | Scenario | Who Declares SupportsShouldProcess | Who Calls ShouldProcess |
  |----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
  | Function does not modify state | Not needed | Not applicable |
  | Function directly modifies state | Public function | Public function |
  | Private function called by one public | Public function | Public function (passes `$PSCmdlet`) |
  | Private function called by multiple publics | Private function | Private function |
  | When unsure about caller count | Private function | Private function |

- Use terminating errors (throw) when the function cannot produce valid output or continue safely. Use non-terminating errors (Write-Error) when the function is processing multiple items and can continue with remaining items.
- Public functions must document their output type using [OutputType()] and return strongly-typed objects where possible.
- Class constructors must validate required parameters and throw [System.ArgumentException] for invalid input. Class methods follow the same error handling rules as functions (terminating vs non-terminating).

- **Function Naming**
  - Before naming a function, verify the name does not conflict with commands in PowerShell built-in modules (Microsoft.PowerShell.*), Pester, or PSScriptAnalyzer. If a conflict exists, add a more specific noun. If you cannot confirm whether a name conflicts, add a code comment: # TODO: Verify this function name does not conflict with existing modules. Additionally, inform the user in your response that the function name could not be verified against existing modules and should be manually checked.
  - All Public functions are to have the noun portion of the name, prefixed with MA (e.g., Build-MAModule).
  - All Private functions have a name with no MA prefix before the noun portion (e.g., Build-Module).
  - The PowerShell filename is to be the same as the function name.
  - When renaming a public function, treat it as a removal of the old name and addition of the new name: remove old files, create new files, and add both a Removed and an Added entry in CHANGELOG.md. Ensure the functions Pester test, if it exists, is also updated and the file renamed.

- **Class Naming**
  - All PowerShell classes must use PascalCase and be prefixed with MA (e.g., MAModuleConfig).
  - The PowerShell classes filename must contain the name of the class, prefixed with a two digit integer and hyphen.  The integer is to provide sort order required for class dependencies to fuction (e.g., 01-MAMake, 02-MAModel, 03-MACar). When creating new class files, you MAY leave gaps in numbering (e.g., 01, 03, 05) to reduce future renumbering. Gaps are always acceptable in existing files. If inserting between consecutive numbers (e.g., 01 and 02), renumber the later file and all subsequent files upward by 1, then insert the new file.
  - When renaming a class, rename its file accordingly and update CHANGELOG.md with Removed/Added entries. When removing a class, remove its file and renumber subsequent files only if the gap between adjacent files exceeds 10. When a class dependency changes requiring reorder, renumber affected files.
  - When renaming or modifying a class's public interface, update all references in functions and other classes that depend on it.

- **Pester Tests**
  - Every new public function must have a corresponding Unit test file at tests/Unit/<FunctionName>.Tests.ps1. When modifying an existing function's behavior, update its tests to cover the new behavior. If an existing function does not have a test, ask the developer in the Chat if they would like to have one added.
  - When removing a public function, also remove its test file.
  - Private functions do not require dedicated test files but must be covered indirectly through the public function tests that call them.
  - When modifying a shared private function, review and update existing tests of all public functions that call it to ensure coverage of the changed behavior.

- **Change Log**
  - When adding or modifying functionality, add an entry under the [Unreleased] section of CHANGELOG.md following keepachangelog format. Categorize entries as Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, or Security.
  - When removing a public function, add a Removed entry to CHANGELOG.md.
  - CHANGELOG entries are required for all user-facing changes. Internal refactors (private functions, classes) only need entries if they change observable behavior of public functions.
  - Each distinct change gets its own bullet entry. Do not combine unrelated changes into a single entry.
  - Changes to templates in src/resources/ require a CHANGELOG entry under Changed or Fixed if they affect generated output.

## Copilot Guidance

- Anytime you require, you are permitted to read any additional files in the code base or the entire code base.
- If uncertain make sure to validate your assumptions against official and current PowerShell or PowerShell Module documentation.

### Output Directives

- DO NOT include introductory remarks (e.g., "Here is the code:") or concluding summaries (e.g., "Let me know if you have questions.").
- DO NOT add superfluous explanations or platitudes about "best practices".
- ONLY output the raw, functional code block or the direct answer to the prompt.
- IF an error occurs, provide only the corrected code and a single paragraph explaining the root cause.
